36	# Lean Auditor
37	
38	You read **every `.lean` file in the project** and produce a per-file checklist of outdated comments, suspect definitions, dead-end proofs, and bad Lean practices. You are **read-only** and your only writable target is your own report under `task_results/`.
39	
40	You are **highly recommended in the review phase**: the review agent dispatches you whenever any `.lean` file has been modified this iter, OR whenever the prior verdict had live must-fix findings. Your output flags issues the prover and plan agents may have missed.
41	
42	## Stance: no strategic bias
43	
44	You audit the Lean **as Lean**. Your directive will NOT include the project strategy, the blueprint, or "what we are trying to prove" — and that is intentional. The point is to detect mathematically-wrong definitions and dead-end proofs *without* being swayed by what the project assumes should be true. A definition is wrong if it is wrong by reading the Lean alone (e.g. its body contradicts standard mathematical usage of the named concept); you do not need to know the strategy to spot that.
45	
46	This stance is also what makes you **critical of excuse-comments**. Lean-side comments like:
47	
48	- `-- We decided to use a temporary wrong definition; will fix it later.`
49	- `-- This proof is incomplete but works for now.`
50	- `-- TODO: replace before merging.`
51	- `-- Stand-in definition until we figure out the right one.`
52	
53	are RED FLAGS, not project workflow. Authors writing such comments are admitting the code is wrong; the comments cannot be allowed to silence the alarm. Flag every such comment with severity **major** at minimum — and **critical** when the named declaration is load-bearing.
54	
55	## Directive Format
56	
57	```markdown
58	# Lean Auditor Directive
59	
60	## Slug
61	<slug>
62	
63	## Scope (files)
64	<"all" by default. Optionally a list of file paths if the review agent wants to narrow scope; never expand beyond the project.>
65	
66	## Focus areas (optional)
67	<directories or namespaces to pay extra attention to. Bias toward thoroughness — do not skip the rest of the project just because focus is named.>
68	
69	## Known issues
70	<things the review agent already knows and doesn't want re-reported>
71	```
72	
73	The directive **does not** include STRATEGY.md, PROGRESS.md, references, or a description of what the project is trying to prove. That is by design.
74	
75	## What you do
76	
77	1. Read your directive.
78	2. **List every `.lean` file** under the project. Use `find` or `ls -R` — do not rely on import graphs (some files may be temporarily orphaned).
79	3. **For each file** (every one, no scope shortcuts):
80	   - Read the whole file.
81	   - For each declaration: is the signature reasonable as Lean? Is the body suspect (uses `axiom`, `Classical.choice _`, `sorry` with a body that looks like fake content, `:= True`, `:= rfl` on a non-trivial claim)?
82	   - For each comment: is it stale (referencing removed code or old strategies)? Is it an *excuse-comment* (see above)? Is it accurate about the code below it?
83	   - For each proof: is the tactic chain reasonable for the goal type, or does it look like it is going in the wrong direction (e.g. unfolding to manipulate syntax instead of using the available structure lemmas)?
84	   - Note any bad Lean practices: misuse of universe polymorphism, redundant typeclasses, deprecated tactics, anti-patterns like proving `False`-style lemmas to bypass infrastructure.
85	4. **Produce a per-file checklist** + a flagged-issues block grouped by severity.
86	
87	You may use:
88	- `archon-lean-lsp` MCP tools (read-only): hover, signature lookup, diagnostics. Useful to verify a declaration's actual type.
89	- `sorry_analyzer` if available, to get sorry counts per file.
90	- Standard `Read` / `Grep` for code reading.
91	
92	You may NOT modify any project file. Even when a fix is obvious.
93	
94	## Report format
95	
96	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/lean-auditor-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path under `task_results/<parent-slug>/` when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
97

100

102	<slug>
103	
104	## Iteration
105	<NNN>
106	
107	## Scope
108	- files audited: <N>
109	- files skipped (per directive): <N> — <reason>
110	
111	## Per-file checklist
112	
113	### <path/to/file.lean>
114	- **outdated comments**: <N flagged> (or "none")
115	- **suspect definitions**: <N flagged> (or "none")
116	- **dead-end proofs**: <N flagged> (or "none")
117	- **bad practices**: <N flagged> (or "none")
118	- **excuse-comments**: <N flagged> (or "none")
119	- **notes**:
120	  - <one bullet per finding, naming line number where relevant>
121	  - ...
122	
123	### <next file>
124	- ...
125	
126	(One block per file. Cover every file, including the ones with no findings — they get `none` everywhere and `notes: -`.)
127	
128	## Must-fix-this-iter
129	
130	Apply verbatim. Every one of the following lands here automatically, no exceptions, no under-classification:
131	
132	- **Excuse-comments** on any declaration: `-- TODO replace with real def`, `-- placeholder`, `-- temporary`, `-- wrong but works`, `-- will fix later`. These are admissions that the code is wrong; treat them as wrong.
133	- **Weakened-wrong definitions**: a `def Foo := <something structurally different>` standing in for the real concept (e.g. `Scheme.LineBundle := CommRing.Pic …` when the real definition is an invertible quasi-coherent sheaf).
134	- **Parallel APIs of existing Mathlib**: a project file that copy-and-modifies a Mathlib definition (changing one type-class or argument) instead of instancing the existing Mathlib version. Bridge lemmas needed to consume it are the symptom.
135	- **Suspect bodies on substantive claims**: `:= sorry` on a load-bearing claim, `:= True`, `:= rfl` on a non-trivial statement, `:= Classical.choice _` without authorization.
136	- **Axioms** on non-trivial claims that the project hasn't explicitly authorized in the strategy.
137	
138	A finding that meets any of the above lands at must-fix-this-iter — even if classifying it as "major" would feel more diplomatic. Soft severity is how wrong code hardens into the project.
139	
140	- `<file>:<line>` — <description>. Why must-fix: <one line>.
141	- ...
142	
143	## Major
144	
145	Findings that are real issues but don't meet the must-fix bar — typically stale comments that don't actively mislead, naming drift, minor code smells.

148	- ...

153	
154	- `<file>:<line>` — <description>.
155	- ...
156	
157	## Excuse-comments (always called out separately)
158	
159	If any excuse-comments were flagged, list each one verbatim with its file:line. These deserve special visibility because they document the project lying to itself.
160	
161	- `Foo/Bar.lean:42`: "TODO: replace with the real definition" (attached to `Foo.bar`, which is a load-bearing definition in `Baz.lean`). Severity: critical.
162	- ...
163	
164	## Severity summary
165	
166	- **must-fix-this-iter**: <N> — these block downstream work in their files until addressed (see plan.md's per-file gate).
167	- **major**: <N>
168	- **minor**: <N>
169	- **excuse-comments**: <N> (also counted under must-fix-this-iter above; called out separately because they document the project lying to itself).
170	
171	Overall verdict: one sentence.
172	```
173	
174	**Strict severity reminder.** The plan agent's gate treats must-fix-this-iter as blocking. If you under-classify a wrong-decision finding to keep the loop's momentum, you are working against the project — wrong code accumulates faster than reviewers can catch it later. When in doubt, classify higher, not lower.
175	
176	## Return value
177	
178	Your final assistant message:
179	
180	- One line: `<slug>: <overall verdict> — <N> files audited, <M> issues (critical/major/minor: a/b/c)`
181	- The path to your full report.
182	
183	## Reminders
184	
185	- **Read every file. No scope shortcuts.**
186	- **No strategic context.** Your audit is unbiased by design.
187	- **You are read-only.** No project source, no blueprint, no state files (except your own report).
188	- **Excuse-comments are red flags.** Critical or major severity, every time.
189	- **Per-file checklist is the primary output.** Cover every file; the format is fixed.

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |

62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |

70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.

111	## Agent Roles

113	### Plan Agent

133	/-! ## §1. The substrate tensor-product operation -/
134	
135	/-- **The substrate operation `⊗` on `Scheme.Modules X`.**
136	
137	For a scheme `X` and `M, N : X.Modules`, the tensor product
138	`M ⊗_X N : X.Modules` is the sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor
139	product `PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` of the underlying presheaves of
140	`M` and `N` (affine-locally `(M ⊗_X N)(Spec A) = M(Spec A) ⊗_A N(Spec A)`).
141	
142	Per blueprint `def:scheme_modules_tensorobj`. The body lifts
143	`PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` through the sheafification functor on
144	the small Zariski site of `X` (fully defined, no `sorry`). -/
145	noncomputable def tensorObj {X : Scheme.{u}} (M N : X.Modules) : X.Modules :=
146	  ((PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := X.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).obj
147	      (PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj (R := X.presheaf) M.val N.val) :
148	    SheafOfModules X.ringCatSheaf)
149	
150	/-- **Functoriality of `⊗_X`.**
151	
152	A pair of morphisms `f : M ⟶ M'` and `g : N ⟶ N'` in `X.Modules` determines a
153	morphism `f ⊗ g : tensorObj M N ⟶ tensorObj M' N'`, compatible with identities
154	and composition; the assignment `(M, N) ↦ tensorObj M N` thereby extends to a
155	bifunctor `X.Modules × X.Modules ⥤ X.Modules` natural in both arguments.
156	
157	Per blueprint `lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality`. The body inherits
158	the morphism action from `PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` under
159	sheafification (fully defined, no `sorry`). -/
160	noncomputable def tensorObj_functoriality {X : Scheme.{u}} {M M' N N' : X.Modules}
161	    (f : M ⟶ M') (g : N ⟶ N') : tensorObj M N ⟶ tensorObj M' N' :=
162	  (PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := X.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).map
163	    (MonoidalCategory.tensorHom
164	      (C := _root_.PresheafOfModules (X.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)) f.val g.val)
165	
166	/-- **`⊗`-invertibility of an `𝒪_X`-module.** (Blueprint
167	`def:scheme_modules_isinvertible`.) `M : X.Modules` is `⊗`-invertible when it
168	admits a tensor inverse: an object `N` with `M ⊗_X N ≅ 𝒪_X`, where
169	`𝒪_X = SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf` is the designated unit. This is the
170	scheme-level analogue of Mathlib's `Module.Invertible`; the predicate carries its
171	inverse witness existentially, so the dual needed by the relative Picard group
172	law is definitional. -/
173	def IsInvertible {X : Scheme.{u}} (M : X.Modules) : Prop :=
174	  ∃ N : X.Modules, Nonempty (tensorObj M N ≅ SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf)
175	
176	/-- **The sheaf-level dual `M^∨ := ℋom_{𝒪_X}(M, 𝒪_X)`** of an `𝒪_X`-module.
177	
178	For a scheme `X` and `M : X.Modules`, the dual `dual M : X.Modules` is the
179	sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules dual `PresheafOfModules.dual` of the
180	underlying presheaf of `M` (the internal hom into the structure presheaf,
181	`M^∨(U) = ℋom_{𝒪_X|_U}(M|_U, 𝒪_X|_U)`).
182	
183	Construction = the **exact dual analogue of `tensorObj`** (this file, `tensorObj`):
184	apply the sheafification functor `PresheafOfModules.sheafification (𝟙 …)` on the
185	small Zariski site of `X` to the (axiom-clean, sub-step-3) presheaf dual
186	`PresheafOfModules.dual M.val`. The scheme's structure presheaf `X.presheaf` is
187	`CommRingCat`-valued over the single-universe topological site `Opens X`, hence is
188	exactly the base `R₀ : Dᵒᵖ ⥤ CommRingCat.{u}` that `PresheafOfModules.dual`
189	requires (the value `M^∨(U) = M|_U ⟶ R|_U` is an `R(U)`-module, needing
190	commutativity) — no CommRingCat/RingCat re-bridging is needed, since
191	`tensorObj` already takes `(R := X.presheaf)` over the same CommRingCat presheaf
192	and `X.ringCatSheaf.val = X.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat` definitionally.
193	
194	The sheafification functor already lands in `SheafOfModules`, so no manual
195	`Presheaf.IsSheaf` / sheaf-condition descent is needed (sheafifying an already-sheaf
196	gives an iso object; this is the file's convention, matching `tensorObj`).
197	
198	Per blueprint `lem:internal_hom_isSheaf` (§`sec:tensorobj_dual_infra`); Stacks
199	tags 01CM (internal hom into a sheaf is a sheaf) / 01CR item 2. This is the
200	`⊗`-inverse candidate of an invertible sheaf, feeding `exists_tensorObj_inverse`. -/
201	noncomputable def dual {X : Scheme.{u}} (M : X.Modules) : X.Modules :=
202	  ((PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := X.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).obj
203	      (PresheafOfModules.dual (R₀ := X.presheaf) M.val) :
204	    SheafOfModules X.ringCatSheaf)
205	
206	/-- **The sheaf-level dual is contravariantly functorial in isomorphisms.** An isomorphism
207	`e : M ≅ M'` in `X.Modules` induces `dual M' ≅ dual M`, obtained by sheafifying the presheaf-level
208	dual iso `PresheafOfModules.dualIsoOfIso` of the underlying presheaf isomorphism. This is the
209	reusable "dual respects isos" ingredient (the dual analogue of `tensorObjIsoOfIso`) feeding the
210	assembly of `dual_isLocallyTrivial`: a trivialisation `L.restrict f ≅ 𝒪` yields, contravariantly,
211	`dual 𝒪 ≅ dual (L.restrict f)`. -/
212	noncomputable def dualIsoOfIso {X : Scheme.{u}} {M M' : X.Modules} (e : M ≅ M') :
213	    dual M' ≅ dual M :=
214	  (PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := X.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).mapIso
215	    (PresheafOfModules.dualIsoOfIso (R₀ := X.presheaf)
216	      ((SheafOfModules.forget X.ringCatSheaf).mapIso e))
217

219	
220	iter-206 PIVOT: the full `MonoidalCategory (X.Modules)` instance is
221	**deliberately not built**. Per blueprint `rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path`,
222	the relative Picard group law is a structure on *isomorphism classes* of line
223	bundles — every group axiom is a `Nonempty (… ≅ …)` proposition, so no monoidal

232	/-! ## §3. The lift through `LineBundle.OnProduct` (PUSH-BEYOND supporting lemmas)
233	
234	The following helper lemmas record the lift of the substrate to the
235	locally-trivial subcategory used by the relative Picard consumer. They are not
236	`\lean{...}`-pinned in the blueprint (their statements are descriptive); the
237	typed signatures here scaffold the iter-203+ bodies. -/
238	
239	/-- **The substrate operation respects isomorphisms in both arguments.**
240	
241	A pair of isomorphisms `e : M ≅ M'` and `e' : N ≅ N'` in `X.Modules` induces an
242	isomorphism `tensorObj M N ≅ tensorObj M' N'`, obtained by sheafifying the
243	tensor product (in the presheaf-of-modules monoidal category) of the underlying
244	presheaf isomorphisms `e.val`, `e'.val`. Its `hom` is
245	`tensorObj_functoriality e.hom e'.hom`. This is the reusable functor-of-isos
246	ingredient feeding `tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial`. -/
247	noncomputable def tensorObjIsoOfIso {X : Scheme.{u}} {M M' N N' : X.Modules}
248	    (e : M ≅ M') (e' : N ≅ N') : tensorObj M N ≅ tensorObj M' N' :=
249	  (PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := X.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).mapIso
250	    (MonoidalCategory.tensorIso
251	      (C := _root_.PresheafOfModules (X.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat))
252	      ((SheafOfModules.forget X.ringCatSheaf).mapIso e)
253	      ((SheafOfModules.forget X.ringCatSheaf).mapIso e'))
254	
255	/-- **The substrate tensor of the structure sheaf with itself is the structure
256	sheaf.**
257	
258	`tensorObj 𝒪_X 𝒪_X ≅ 𝒪_X`, where `𝒪_X = SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf` is the
259	designated unit object. Built from the presheaf-level left unitor
260	`λ_ (𝟙_)` (the unit of the `PresheafOfModules` monoidal category is exactly
261	`SheafOfModules.unit.val`) under sheafification, composed with the
262	sheafification-adjunction counit isomorphism on the (already-sheaf) unit. -/
263	noncomputable def tensorObj_unit_iso {X : Scheme.{u}} :
264	    tensorObj (SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf) (SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf)
265	      ≅ SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf :=
266	  (PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := X.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).mapIso
267	      (λ_ (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules (X.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)))) ≪≫
268	    (asIso (PresheafOfModules.sheafificationAdjunction (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).counit).app
269	      (SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf)
270	
271	/-- **Left unitor for `⊗_X`.** `𝒪_X ⊗_X M ≅ M`. (Blueprint
272	`lem:tensorobj_unit_iso`, left half, `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_left_unitor`.)
273	Sheafification of the presheaf-level left unitor `λ_ M.val`, composed with the
274	sheafification counit identifying `sheafification M.val` with the (already-sheaf)
275	`M`. The cheap `mapIso` pattern; uses no abstract pullback. -/
276	noncomputable def tensorObj_left_unitor {X : Scheme.{u}} (M : X.Modules) :
277	    tensorObj (SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf) M ≅ M :=
278	  (PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := X.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).mapIso
279	      ((PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategoryStruct (R := X.presheaf)).leftUnitor M.val) ≪≫
280	    (asIso (PresheafOfModules.sheafificationAdjunction (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).counit).app M
281	
282	/-- **Right unitor for `⊗_X`.** `M ⊗_X 𝒪_X ≅ M`. (Blueprint
283	`lem:tensorobj_unit_iso`, right half, `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_right_unitor`.)

285	sheafification counit. -/
286	noncomputable def tensorObj_right_unitor {X : Scheme.{u}} (M : X.Modules) :
287	    tensorObj M (SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf) ≅ M :=
288	  (PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := X.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).mapIso
289	      ((PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategoryStruct (R := X.presheaf)).rightUnitor M.val) ≪≫
290	    (asIso (PresheafOfModules.sheafificationAdjunction (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).counit).app M
291	
292	/-- **Braiding for `⊗_X`.** `M ⊗_X N ≅ N ⊗_X M`. (Blueprint
293	`lem:tensorobj_comm_iso`, `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_braiding`.)
294	The presheaf-of-modules monoidal category is symmetric; its braiding `β_ M.val
295	N.val` sheafifies to the asserted isomorphism by the cheap `mapIso` pattern. -/

701	/-! ## §5. The invertibility-carrier Picard group `picCommGroup`
702	
703	This is the by-hand commutative-group law on isomorphism classes of `⊗`-invertible
704	`𝒪_X`-modules (blueprint §`sec:tensorobj_pic_carrier`). Every group axiom is a single
705	existence-of-isomorphism `Nonempty (… ≅ …)` read as an equality of iso-classes; no
706	pentagon/triangle/hexagon coherence and no `MonoidalCategory` instance is invoked.
707	The inverse is carried by the membership witness of `IsInvertible` itself. -/
708	
709	/-- **Step 1 — associator on `⊗`-invertible objects** (blueprint
710	`lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso_invertible`). An immediate specialisation of the now
711	*unconditional* `tensorObj_assoc_iso`; the invertibility hypotheses are not consumed
712	(they match the blueprint statement). -/
713	noncomputable def tensorObj_assoc_iso_invertible {X : Scheme.{u}} {M N P : X.Modules}
714	    (_hM : IsInvertible M) (_hN : IsInvertible N) (_hP : IsInvertible P) :
715	    tensorObj (tensorObj M N) P ≅ tensorObj M (tensorObj N P) :=
716	  tensorObj_assoc_iso
717	
718	/-- **Middle-four interchange for `⊗_X`** (helper). For arbitrary `𝒪_X`-modules
719	`A, B, C, D`, there is an isomorphism `(A ⊗ B) ⊗ (C ⊗ D) ≅ (A ⊗ C) ⊗ (B ⊗ D)`,
720	assembled from the unconditional associator and the braiding (no coherence consumed).
721	Used to reassociate the four factors in `IsInvertible.tensorObj`. -/
722	private noncomputable def tensorObj_middleFour {X : Scheme.{u}} (A B C D : X.Modules) :
723	    tensorObj (tensorObj A B) (tensorObj C D)
724	      ≅ tensorObj (tensorObj A C) (tensorObj B D) :=
725	  tensorObj_assoc_iso ≪≫
726	    tensorObjIsoOfIso (Iso.refl A)
727	      (tensorObj_assoc_iso.symm ≪≫
728	        tensorObjIsoOfIso (tensorObj_braiding B C) (Iso.refl D) ≪≫
729	        tensorObj_assoc_iso) ≪≫
730	    tensorObj_assoc_iso.symm
731	
732	/-- **Step 3 — `⊗`-invertibility is closed under tensor product** (blueprint
733	`lem:isinvertible_tensor`). If `M, M'` are `⊗`-invertible with inverses `N, N'`,
734	then `N ⊗ N'` is a tensor inverse of `M ⊗ M'`. -/
735	theorem IsInvertible.tensorObj {X : Scheme.{u}} {M M' : X.Modules}
736	    (hM : IsInvertible M) (hM' : IsInvertible M') :
737	    IsInvertible (Scheme.Modules.tensorObj M M') := by
738	  obtain ⟨N, ⟨e⟩⟩ := hM
739	  obtain ⟨N', ⟨e'⟩⟩ := hM'
740	  exact ⟨Scheme.Modules.tensorObj N N',
741	    ⟨tensorObj_middleFour M M' N N' ≪≫ tensorObjIsoOfIso e e' ≪≫ tensorObj_unit_iso⟩⟩

744	`lem:isinvertible_unit`). Witness `𝒪_X`, iso `tensorObj_unit_iso`. -/

746	    IsInvertible (SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf) :=
747	  ⟨SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf, ⟨tensorObj_unit_iso⟩⟩

750	`lem:isinvertible_inverse_welldef`). If `M ⊗ N ≅ 𝒪_X` and `M ⊗ N' ≅ 𝒪_X` then
751	`N ≅ N'`, via the inverse-of-inverse chain. -/
752	theorem IsInvertible.inverse_unique {X : Scheme.{u}} {M N N' : X.Modules}
753	    (e : Scheme.Modules.tensorObj M N ≅ SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf)
754	    (e' : Scheme.Modules.tensorObj M N' ≅ SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf) :
755	    Nonempty (N ≅ N') :=
756	  ⟨(tensorObj_right_unitor N).symm ≪≫
757	    tensorObjIsoOfIso (Iso.refl N) e'.symm ≪≫
758	    tensorObj_assoc_iso.symm ≪≫
759	    tensorObjIsoOfIso (tensorObj_braiding N M ≪≫ e) (Iso.refl N') ≪≫
760	    tensorObj_left_unitor N'⟩
761	
762	/-- The setoid of `⊗`-invertible `𝒪_X`-modules: `M ∼ M'` iff there exists an
763	isomorphism `M ≅ M'` (blueprint `def:pic_carrier`). -/
764	instance picSetoid (X : Scheme.{u}) : Setoid {M : X.Modules // IsInvertible M} where
765	  r M M' := Nonempty ((M : X.Modules) ≅ (M' : X.Modules))
766	  iseqv :=
767	    ⟨fun _ => ⟨Iso.refl _⟩, fun ⟨e⟩ => ⟨e.symm⟩, fun ⟨e⟩ ⟨f⟩ => ⟨e ≪≫ f⟩⟩

770	`def:pic_carrier`): the quotient of `⊗`-invertible `𝒪_X`-modules by isomorphism. -/
771	def PicGroup (X : Scheme.{u}) : Type _ := Quotient (picSetoid X)

775	`IsInvertible.tensorObj`. -/
776	noncomputable def picMul {X : Scheme.{u}} : PicGroup X → PicGroup X → PicGroup X :=
777	  Quotient.lift₂
778	    (fun a b => Quotient.mk _ ⟨tensorObj a.1 b.1, a.2.tensorObj b.2⟩)
779	    (by
780	      rintro ⟨a, ha⟩ ⟨b, hb⟩ ⟨a', ha'⟩ ⟨b', hb'⟩ ⟨ea⟩ ⟨eb⟩
781	      exact Quotient.sound ⟨tensorObjIsoOfIso ea eb⟩)

786	  Quotient.lift
787	    (fun a => Quotient.mk _
788	      ⟨Classical.choose a.2,
789	        a.1, ⟨tensorObj_braiding _ a.1 ≪≫ (Classical.choose_spec a.2).some⟩⟩)
790	    (by
791	      rintro ⟨a, ha⟩ ⟨a', ha'⟩ ⟨ea⟩
792	      refine Quotient.sound ?_

795	        (Classical.choose_spec ha).some

1215	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — D1: the presheaf pullback Lan decomposition
1216	
1217	These are general `PresheafOfModules` statements (any `F : C ⥤ D`, ring presheaves
1218	`R, S`), not specific to schemes; they are project-local because Mathlib's pinned commit
1219	exposes neither `extendScalars` nor `pullback₀` at the presheaf-of-modules level. Both are
1220	realised as the left adjoint of an existing right adjoint: `pushforward₀ F R` is
1221	definitionally `pushforward (𝟙 (F.op ⋙ R))` (because `restrictScalars (𝟙) = 𝟭` on the nose,
1222	witnessed by Mathlib's `restrictScalars (𝟙 R)).Full := inferInstanceAs (𝟭 _).Full`), and
1223	`restrictScalars φ` is definitionally `pushforward (F := 𝟭) φ`; since `pushforward _` is
1224	always a right adjoint, so are these two, and their left adjoints `pullback₀`/`extendScalars`
1225	exist. The decomposition `pullback φ ≅ extendScalars φ ⋙ pullback₀` then follows from the
1226	definitional factorisation `pushforward φ = pushforward₀ F R ⋙ restrictScalars φ` by
1227	uniqueness of left adjoints (`Adjunction.leftAdjointCompIso`).
1228	
1229	Per blueprint `lem:pullback_lan_decomposition` (D1). **OFF-PATH (iter-243 pivot).** D1 is
1230	axiom-clean and self-contained, but it was the first brick of the ABANDONED general
1231	strong-monoidal pullback build (`sec:tensorobj_pullback_monoidality`, route (e)); the active
1232	route is the loc-triv chart-chase D1'–D4' (§below), which does NOT consume `extendScalars`/
1233	`pullback₀`. Retained as a correct, reusable presheaf-level decomposition; do NOT extend it
1234	toward the general build (D2 scalar-strong / D3 topological-interchange are NOT pursued). -/
1235	
1236	section PullbackLanDecomposition
1237	
1238	variable {C : Type u} [Category.{u} C] {D : Type u} [Category.{u} D]
1239	  {F : C ⥤ D} {R : Dᵒᵖ ⥤ RingCat.{u}} {S : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ RingCat.{u}}
1240	
1241	/-- `pushforward₀ F R` is a right adjoint: it is definitionally `pushforward (𝟙 (F.op ⋙ R))`
1242	(since `restrictScalars (𝟙) = 𝟭` on the nose). Project-local; carries the existence of the
1243	topological inverse image `pullback₀`. -/
1244	private lemma pushforward₀IsRightAdjoint (F : C ⥤ D) (R : Dᵒᵖ ⥤ RingCat.{u}) :
1245	    (PresheafOfModules.pushforward₀.{u} F R).IsRightAdjoint :=
1246	  inferInstanceAs (PresheafOfModules.pushforward.{u} (𝟙 (F.op ⋙ R))).IsRightAdjoint
1247	
1248	/-- `restrictScalars φ` is a right adjoint: it is definitionally `pushforward (F := 𝟭) φ`.
1249	Project-local; carries the existence of the extension of scalars `extendScalars`. -/
1250	private lemma restrictScalarsIsRightAdjoint (φ : S ⟶ F.op ⋙ R) :
1251	    (PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars.{u} φ).IsRightAdjoint :=
1252	  inferInstanceAs
1253	    (PresheafOfModules.pushforward.{u} (F := 𝟭 C) (R := F.op ⋙ R) φ).IsRightAdjoint
1254	
1255	/-- **The topological inverse image `pullback₀ := (pushforward₀ F R).leftAdjoint`**, the
1256	left adjoint of the fixed-ring pushforward. On underlying presheaves it is the left Kan
1257	extension along `F.op`. Project-local (no `PresheafOfModules` inverse-image functor at the
1258	pin); the carrier of D3. -/
1259	noncomputable def pullback0 (F : C ⥤ D) (R : Dᵒᵖ ⥤ RingCat.{u}) :
1260	    _root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (F.op ⋙ R) ⥤ _root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} R :=
1261	  haveI := pushforward₀IsRightAdjoint F R
1262	  (PresheafOfModules.pushforward₀ F R).leftAdjoint
1263	
1264	/-- **Extension of scalars `extendScalars φ := (restrictScalars φ).leftAdjoint`**, the left
1265	adjoint of restriction of scalars along a morphism of ring presheaves. Project-local; the
1266	carrier of D2 (strong monoidal via `distribBaseChange`). -/
1267	noncomputable def extendScalars (φ : S ⟶ F.op ⋙ R) :
1268	    _root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} S ⥤ _root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (F.op ⋙ R) :=
1269	  haveI := restrictScalarsIsRightAdjoint φ
1270	  (PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars φ).leftAdjoint
1271	
1272	/-- The adjunction `pullback₀ ⊣ pushforward₀`. -/
1273	noncomputable def pullback0Adjunction (F : C ⥤ D) (R : Dᵒᵖ ⥤ RingCat.{u}) :
1274	    pullback0 F R ⊣ PresheafOfModules.pushforward₀ F R :=
1275	  haveI := pushforward₀IsRightAdjoint F R
1276	  Adjunction.ofIsRightAdjoint (PresheafOfModules.pushforward₀ F R)
1277	
1278	/-- The adjunction `extendScalars φ ⊣ restrictScalars φ`. -/
1279	noncomputable def extendScalarsAdjunction (φ : S ⟶ F.op ⋙ R) :
1280	    extendScalars φ ⊣ PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars φ :=
1281	  haveI := restrictScalarsIsRightAdjoint φ
1282	  Adjunction.ofIsRightAdjoint (PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars φ)
1283	
1284	/-- **D1 — the presheaf pullback Lan decomposition** (blueprint `lem:pullback_lan_decomposition`).
1285	For `φ : S ⟶ F.op ⋙ R` presenting a pushforward of presheaves of modules, the presheaf
1286	pullback factors as extension of scalars followed by the topological inverse image,
1287	`pullback φ ≅ extendScalars φ ⋙ pullback₀`. This is the left-adjoint reversal of the
1288	definitional factorisation `pushforward φ = pushforward₀ F R ⋙ restrictScalars φ`, obtained
1289	from `Adjunction.leftAdjointCompIso` (uniqueness of left adjoints). Project-local. -/
1290	noncomputable def pullbackLanDecomposition (φ : S ⟶ F.op ⋙ R) :
1291	    PresheafOfModules.pullback φ ≅ extendScalars φ ⋙ pullback0 F R :=
1292	  (Adjunction.leftAdjointCompIso
1293	    (extendScalarsAdjunction φ) (pullback0Adjunction F R)
1294	    (PresheafOfModules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction φ)
1295	    (Iso.refl (PresheafOfModules.pushforward φ))).symm
1296	
1297	end PullbackLanDecomposition
